concept: University costs six things, not one — and only one of them is on the website.

hook.fact: Two students. Same university. Same tuition. One pays $10,000 more per year.
hook.question: Why?

conceptCards.title: Card 1
conceptCards.body: Tuition is the price on the website. It is not the cost of going. The real figure — called **Total Cost of Attendance (TCA)** — includes everything that leaves your account while you are a student.

conceptCards.title: Card 2
conceptCards.body: Students who plan only for tuition regularly run out of money by their second semester. TCA exists so that does not happen to you.

conceptCards.title: Card 3
conceptCards.body: TCA has six categories: tuition, accommodation, food, transport, course materials, and personal spending. Together, these are your real annual cost.

conceptCards.title: Card 4
conceptCards.body: Your behaviour controls some of these categories. Tuition is fixed. Rent can be negotiated. Food can be managed. Personal spending is almost entirely up to you.

conceptCards.title: Card 5
conceptCards.body: Now test this: A three-year degree that takes four years does not cost 33% more — it costs 40–50% more once you add a full extra year of living costs. Always plan for this possibility.

questions.explanation: "TCA stands for Total Cost of Attendance — the full annual cost, not just tuition."

questions.statement: Tuition is the only cost you need to plan for at university.
questions.whyPrompt: Why is that true or false?
questions.explanation: "Tuition is one of six categories — accommodation, food, transport, materials, and personal spending all add to the real total."

questions.prompt: Match each TCA category to its example:

pairs.right: Fee paid to the university each term
Monthly rent for your student flat

questions.explanation: Review the concept and try again.

questions.sentence: "The real annual cost of university — covering all six spending categories — is called ___."
questions.explanation: Review the concept and try again.

questions.sentence: Complete the sentence: "A student who saves only for tuition will run short because ___ and ___ are not included — and together they can ___ the real annual cost."
questions.explanation: Review the concept and try again.

questions.scenario: Marcus wrote in his university plan: "I need £12,000 saved. That covers my tuition and I'm set."
options: He saved the wrong amount for tuition
He forgot that accommodation, food, and other living costs will add another
Tuition is not his biggest cost
questions.explanation: "Tuition only covers one of six TCA categories — Marcus has left out a full year of living costs."

tasks.scenario: Sofia is heading to university next year. She has saved exactly the amount her university lists as tuition: £14,000.
tasks.explanation: "UK living costs typically add £10,000–£15,000 per year on top of tuition. Sofia's savings cover less than half her real TCA."

tasks.scenario: Priya's university charges €10,000 tuition. Her rent is €600/month, food costs €300/month, and transport is €100/month.
tasks.prompt: What is her annual TCA (tuition + accommodation + food + transport only)?
tasks.explanation: "€10,000 tuition + €7,200 rent + €3,600 food + €1,200 transport = €22,000. And that's before materials and personal spending."

lockIn.takeaway: TCA is what university actually costs — and it is always higher than the tuition figure alone.

reviewQueueEntry.definition: Total Cost of Attendance (TCA) anchors better education money decisions.
reviewQueueEntry.quickRecallPrompt: What does Total Cost of Attendance (TCA) include beyond tuition?
